    - cd terraform/environments/prod
    - terraform init
    - terraform plan -out=plan.cache
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - terraform/environments/prod/plan.cache
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH

apply-prod:
  stage: apply
  environment:
    name: production
    url: https://www.example.com
  script:
    - cd terraform/environments/prod
    - terraform apply -auto-approve plan.cache
  dependencies:
    - plan-prod
  when: manual
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH
⚠️ Static Credentials Warning: Static AWS credentials (access keys) should only be used when OIDC is not available. They require manual rotation, can be leaked in logs, and provide persistent access. Always prefer OIDC (Section 41.3.1) for production environments. If you must use static credentials: enable Protected (limit to protected branches), enable Masked (hide from logs), and scope to specific environments.

41.4 Complete Pipeline Example

COMPLETE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: GitLab Terraform Pipelines

A comprehensive, production-ready GitLab CI/CD pipeline for Terraform with OIDC authentication, multi-environment support, managed state, MR integration, and manual approval gates.

Production-Ready GitLab Terraform Pipeline
Complete .gitlab-ci.yml with all best practices 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — Production-Ready Terraform Pipeline
#
# Features:
#   ✅ OIDC authentication (keyless AWS access)
#   ✅ GitLab Managed Terraform State
#   ✅ MR plan widget integration
#   ✅ Multi-environment (dev → staging → production)
#   ✅ Manual approval gates for production
#   ✅ Caching for faster pipelines
#   ✅ Security scanning with tfsec
#
# Requirements:
#   - GitLab 15.7+ (for id_tokens)
#   - AWS OIDC provider configured (see Section 41.3.1)
#   - CI/CD Variables: ROLE_ARN_DEV, ROLE_ARN_STAGING, ROLE_ARN_PROD

# ─── Global Configuration ────────────────────────────
variables:
  TF_ROOT: "${CI_PROJECT_DIR}/terraform"
  AWS_DEFAULT_REGION: "us-east-1"
  TERRAFORM_VERSION: "1.7"

image:
  name: registry.gitlab.com/gitlab-org/terraform-images/releases/${TERRAFORM_VERSION}:v1.1.0

cache:
  key: "${TF_ROOT}"
  paths:
    - ${TF_ROOT}/.terraform/providers

stages:
  - validate
  - security
  - plan
  - apply
  - cleanup

# ─── Templates ────────────────────────────────────────
.aws_oidc_auth:
  id_tokens:
    GITLAB_OIDC_TOKEN:
      aud: "https://gitlab.com"
  before_script:
    - >
      STS=$(aws sts assume-role-with-web-identity
      --role-arn ${ROLE_ARN}
      --role-session-name "gitlab-${CI_PIPELINE_ID}"
      --web-identity-token ${GITLAB_OIDC_TOKEN}
      --duration-seconds 3600
      --output json)
    - export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$(echo $STS | jq -r '.Credentials.AccessKeyId')
    - export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$(echo $STS | jq -r '.Credentials.SecretAccessKey')
    - export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=$(echo $STS | jq -r '.Credentials.SessionToken')
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}

.terraform_plan:
  stage: plan
  extends: .aws_oidc_auth
  script:
    - gitlab-terraform init
    - gitlab-terraform plan
    - gitlab-terraform plan-json
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache
    reports:
      terraform: ${TF_ROOT}/plan.json
    expire_in: 7 days

.terraform_apply:
  stage: apply
  extends: .aws_oidc_auth
  script:
    - gitlab-terraform init
    - gitlab-terraform apply
  when: manual

# ─── Validate Stage ──────────────────────────────────
fmt:
  stage: validate
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff
  allow_failure: true

validate:
  stage: validate
  extends: .aws_oidc_auth
  variables:
    ROLE_ARN: ${ROLE_ARN_DEV}
  script:
    - gitlab-terraform init
    - gitlab-terraform validate

# ─── Security Stage ──────────────────────────────────
tfsec:
  stage: security
  image:
    name: aquasec/tfsec:latest
    entrypoint: [""]
  script:
    - tfsec ${TF_ROOT} --format json --out tfsec-report.json || true
    - tfsec ${TF_ROOT} --format lovely
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - tfsec-report.json
    expire_in: 30 days
  allow_failure: true

# ─── Plan Stage ──────────────────────────────────────
plan-dev:
  extends: .terraform_plan
  variables:
    ROLE_ARN: ${ROLE_ARN_DEV}
    TF_STATE_NAME: "dev"
    TF_VAR_environment: "dev"
  environment:
    name: dev
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH

plan-staging:
  extends: .terraform_plan
  variables:
    ROLE_ARN: ${ROLE_ARN_STAGING}
    TF_STATE_NAME: "staging"
    TF_VAR_environment: "staging"
  environment:
    name: staging
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH

plan-prod:
  extends: .terraform_plan
  variables:
    ROLE_ARN: ${ROLE_ARN_PROD}
    TF_STATE_NAME: "prod"
    TF_VAR_environment: "prod"
  environment:
    name: production
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH

# ─── Apply Stage ─────────────────────────────────────
apply-dev:
  extends: .terraform_apply
  variables:
    ROLE_ARN: ${ROLE_ARN_DEV}
    TF_STATE_NAME: "dev"
  dependencies:
    - plan-dev
  environment:
    name: dev
    url: https://dev.example.com
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH

apply-staging:
  extends: .terraform_apply
  variables:
    ROLE_ARN: ${ROLE_ARN_STAGING}
    TF_STATE_NAME: "staging"
  needs: ["plan-staging", "apply-dev"]
  dependencies:
    - plan-staging
  environment:
    name: staging
    url: https://staging.example.com
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH

apply-prod:
  extends: .terraform_apply
  variables:
    ROLE_ARN: ${ROLE_ARN_PROD}
    TF_STATE_NAME: "prod"
  needs: ["plan-prod", "apply-staging"]
  dependencies:
    - plan-prod
  environment:
    name: production
    url: https://www.example.com
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH

# ─── Cleanup Stage ───────────────────────────────────
cleanup-plan-cache:
  stage: cleanup
  script:
    - rm -f ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache
    - echo "Plan cache cleaned up"
  when: always
📋 Pipeline Feature Summary:
FeatureImplementationBenefit
OIDC Auth.aws_oidc_auth template with id_tokensNo static credentials to manage or rotate
Managed StateTF_STATE_NAME per environmentZero external state backend setup
MR Widgetreports: terraform artifactPlan visible in code review
Environment Promotionneeds DAG: dev → staging → prodSequential deployment with gates
Manual Approvalwhen: manual on apply jobsHuman verification before apply
Security Scanningtfsec in security stageCatch misconfigurations early
CachingProvider cache between runsFaster pipeline execution
🏗️ Pipeline Visualization: 
 MR Created                    Merged to Main
     │                              │
     ▼                              ▼
 ┌────────┐                   ┌────────┐
 │validate│                   │validate│
 │ fmt    │                   │ fmt    │
 │ check  │                   │ check  │
 └───┬────┘                   └───┬────┘
     ▼                            ▼
 ┌────────┐                   ┌────────┐
 │security│                   │security│
 │ tfsec  │                   │ tfsec  │
 └───┬────┘                   └───┬────┘
     ▼                            ▼
 ┌────────┐              ┌────────┬────────┬────────┐
 │plan-dev│              │plan-dev│plan-stg│plan-prd│
 │ (MR    │              └───┬────┘───┬────┘───┬────┘
 │widget) │                  ▼        │        │
 └────────┘              ┌────────┐   │        │
                         │apply   │   │        │
                         │-dev    │   │        │
                         │(manual)│   │        │
                         └───┬────┘   ▼        │
                             │   ┌────────┐    │
                             └──▶│apply   │    │
                                 │-staging│    │
                                 │(manual)│    │
                                 └───┬────┘    ▼
                                     │   ┌────────┐
                                     └──▶│apply   │
                                         │-prod   │
                                         │(manual)│
                                         └────────┘
🔧 🏗️ 🚀 ⚙️
CHAPTER 42

Other CI/CD Platforms

Jenkins Azure DevOps CodePipeline CircleCI Atlantis Spacelift env0
7Platforms
20Subsections
15+Examples
🔧Multi-Tool
PART 11 ADVANCED ★★★★★

Explore Terraform integration across major CI/CD platforms — from traditional Jenkins pipelines to modern GitOps tools like Atlantis, Spacelift, and env0. Each platform offers unique approaches to infrastructure automation.

Learning Objectives

  • Build Terraform pipelines in Jenkins using Declarative Pipeline syntax
  • Configure Azure DevOps Pipelines with service connections for cloud deployments
  • Use AWS CodePipeline and CodeBuild for native AWS Terraform automation
  • Set up CircleCI with Terraform orbs for streamlined configuration
  • Deploy Atlantis for PR-based Terraform workflow automation
  • Leverage Spacelift and env0 for enterprise Terraform management

Chapter Contents

42.1 Jenkins 42.2 Azure DevOps Pipelines 42.3 AWS CodePipeline 42.4 CircleCI 42.5 Atlantis 42.6 Spacelift 42.7 env0

42.1 Jenkins

PLATFORM 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Jenkins Pipeline

Jenkins is a self-hosted automation server with a massive plugin ecosystem. Terraform pipelines in Jenkins use Declarative or Scripted Pipeline syntax defined in a Jenkinsfile.

42.1.1 Jenkinsfile

The Jenkinsfile defines your pipeline as code. Use the Declarative Pipeline syntax for Terraform workflows with clear stages and approval gates.

Jenkins Declarative Pipeline for Terraform
Complete Jenkinsfile with plan, approval, and apply stages 
// Jenkinsfile — Terraform Declarative Pipeline
//
// Stages: Checkout → Init → Validate → Plan → Approval → Apply

pipeline {
    agent {
        docker {
            image 'hashicorp/terraform:1.7'
            args  '--entrypoint=""'
        }
    }

    parameters {
        choice(
            name: 'ENVIRONMENT',
            choices: ['dev', 'staging', 'production'],
            description: 'Target environment'
        )
        booleanParam(
            name: 'AUTO_APPROVE',
            defaultValue: false,
            description: 'Skip manual approval (dev only)'
        )
    }

    environment {
        AWS_DEFAULT_REGION = 'us-east-1'
        TF_ROOT           = 'terraform/environments/${params.ENVIRONMENT}'
        TF_IN_AUTOMATION   = 'true'
    }

    stages {
        stage('Checkout') {
            steps {
                checkout scm
            }
        }

        stage('Init') {
            steps {
                dir("${TF_ROOT}") {
                    sh 'terraform init -input=false'
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Validate') {
            steps {
                dir("${TF_ROOT}") {
                    sh 'terraform fmt -check -recursive'
                    sh 'terraform validate'
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Plan') {
            steps {
                dir("${TF_ROOT}") {
                    sh 'terraform plan -input=false -out=tfplan'
                    sh 'terraform show -no-color tfplan > plan_output.txt'
                }
            }
            post {
                always {
                    archiveArtifacts artifacts: "${TF_ROOT}/tfplan"
                    archiveArtifacts artifacts: "${TF_ROOT}/plan_output.txt"
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Approval') {
            when {
                allOf {
                    not { environment name: 'ENVIRONMENT', value: 'dev' }
                    not { expression { params.AUTO_APPROVE } }
                }
            }
            steps {
                script {
                    def planOutput = readFile("${TF_ROOT}/plan_output.txt")
                    input message: "Review Terraform Plan for ${params.ENVIRONMENT}",
                          parameters: [
                              text(name: 'PLAN', defaultValue: planOutput,
                                   description: 'Terraform Plan Output')
                          ]
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Apply') {
            steps {
                dir("${TF_ROOT}") {
                    sh 'terraform apply -input=false -auto-approve tfplan'
                }
            }
        }
    }

    post {
        failure {
            slackSend channel: '#infra-alerts',
                      color: 'danger',
                      message: "❌ Terraform ${params.ENVIRONMENT} failed: ${env.BUILD_URL}"
        }
        success {
            slackSend channel: '#infra-deployments',
                      color: 'good',
                      message: "✅ Terraform ${params.ENVIRONMENT} applied: ${env.BUILD_URL}"
        }
        always {
            cleanWs()
        }
    }
}
42.1.2 Terraform Plugin

The Jenkins Terraform plugin provides native integration with Terraform commands, automatic workspace management, and built-in state file handling.

Using the Terraform Plugin
Pipeline with the Jenkins Terraform plugin steps 
// Jenkinsfile — Using Terraform Plugin
//
// Requires: Terraform Plugin installed in Jenkins
// Config: Manage Jenkins → Tools → Terraform Installations

pipeline {
    agent any

    tools {
        // Configured in Jenkins Global Tool Configuration
        terraform 'terraform-1.7'
    }

    stages {
        stage('Init & Plan') {
            steps {
                dir('terraform') {
                    // Plugin provides 'terraform' step
                    sh 'terraform init -input=false'
                    sh 'terraform plan -input=false -out=tfplan'
                }
            }
        }

        stage('Apply') {
            steps {
                dir('terraform') {
                    sh 'terraform apply -input=false -auto-approve tfplan'
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

// ─── Alternative: Shared Library Approach ─────────
// vars/terraformPipeline.groovy (in shared library)
//
// def call(Map config) {
//     pipeline {
//         agent { docker { image "hashicorp/terraform:${config.version}" } }
//         stages {
//             stage('Init')     { steps { sh "terraform init" } }
//             stage('Plan')     { steps { sh "terraform plan -out=plan" } }
//             stage('Approve')  { steps { input "Apply changes?" } }
//             stage('Apply')    { steps { sh "terraform apply plan" } }
//         }
//     }
// }
//
// // Usage in Jenkinsfile:
// terraformPipeline(version: '1.7')
Jenkins Plugin Purpose Installation
terraformTerraform tool installation & managementManage Jenkins → Plugins
pipeline-awsAWS credential helpers for pipelinesManage Jenkins → Plugins
credentials-bindingInject secrets into pipeline stepsUsually pre-installed
ansiColorColored Terraform output in consoleManage Jenkins → Plugins
42.1.3 Credential Management

Jenkins stores credentials in its built-in credential store. Use withCredentials or withAWS to inject secrets into Terraform pipeline stages.

Jenkins Credential Injection
Injecting AWS credentials into Terraform steps 
// Jenkinsfile — Credential Management
//
// Credentials stored in: Manage Jenkins → Credentials

pipeline {
    agent any

    stages {
        // Option 1: withCredentials binding
        stage('Plan with Credentials') {
            steps {
                withCredentials([
                    [
                        $class: 'AmazonWebServicesCredentialsBinding',
                        credentialsId: 'aws-terraform-prod',
                        accessKeyVariable: 'AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID',
                        secretKeyVariable: 'AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY'
                    ]
                ]) {
                    dir('terraform') {
                        sh 'terraform init'
                        sh 'terraform plan -out=tfplan'
                    }
                }
            }
        }

        // Option 2: withAWS (pipeline-aws plugin)
        stage('Apply with AssumeRole') {
            steps {
                withAWS(
                    credentials: 'aws-terraform-shared',
                    role: 'arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/terraform-deploy',
                    roleSessionName: 'jenkins-terraform'
                ) {
                    dir('terraform') {
                        sh 'terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan'
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
🔒 Jenkins Security Best Practices:
  • Credential Store: Never hardcode secrets in Jenkinsfiles — use the Jenkins credential store
  • Folder Credentials: Scope credentials to specific Jenkins folders for least privilege
  • Role-Based Access: Use the Role Strategy plugin to control who can run Terraform pipelines
  • Audit Trail: Enable the Audit Trail plugin to log all credential access
  • Agent Isolation: Run Terraform in Docker containers to isolate the build environment

42.2 Azure DevOps Pipelines

PLATFORM 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Azure DevOps Pipelines

Azure DevOps Pipelines supports Terraform through YAML-based pipeline definitions, marketplace extensions, and service connections for multi-cloud authentication.

42.2.1 azure-pipelines.yml

Azure DevOps pipelines are defined in YAML files. Use stages, jobs, and tasks to build Terraform workflows with approvals and environment gates.

Azure DevOps Terraform Pipeline
Multi-stage pipeline with plan, approval, and apply 
# azure-pipelines.yml — Terraform Pipeline
#
# Trigger: PR creates plan, merge to main triggers apply

trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main
  paths:
    include:
      - terraform/**

pr:
  branches:
    include:
      - main
  paths:
    include:
      - terraform/**

variables:
  TF_ROOT: 'terraform'
  AWS_REGION: 'us-east-1'
  terraformVersion: '1.7.0'

stages:
  # ─── Validate Stage ────────────────────────────
  - stage: Validate
    displayName: 'Validate Terraform'
    jobs:
      - job: Validate
        pool:
          vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
        steps:
          - task: TerraformInstaller@1
            displayName: 'Install Terraform'
            inputs:
              terraformVersion: $(terraformVersion)

          - script: |
              cd $(TF_ROOT)
              terraform init -backend=false
              terraform fmt -check -recursive
              terraform validate
            displayName: 'Format Check & Validate'

  # ─── Plan Stage ────────────────────────────────
  - stage: Plan
    displayName: 'Terraform Plan'
    dependsOn: Validate
    jobs:
      - job: Plan
        pool:
          vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
        steps:
          - task: TerraformInstaller@1
            inputs:
              terraformVersion: $(terraformVersion)

          - task: AWSShellScript@1
            displayName: 'Terraform Plan'
            inputs:
              awsCredentials: 'aws-service-connection'
              regionName: $(AWS_REGION)
              scriptType: 'inline'
              inlineScript: |
                cd $(TF_ROOT)
                terraform init
                terraform plan -out=tfplan
                terraform show -no-color tfplan > $(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)/plan_output.txt

          - publish: $(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)
            artifact: terraform-plan
            displayName: 'Publish Plan Artifact'

  # ─── Apply Stage ───────────────────────────────
  - stage: Apply
    displayName: 'Terraform Apply'
    dependsOn: Plan
    condition: and(succeeded(), eq(variables['Build.SourceBranch'], 'refs/heads/main'))
    jobs:
      - deployment: Apply
        pool:
          vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
        environment: 'production'    # Requires approval in Azure DevOps
        strategy:
          runOnce:
            deploy:
              steps:
                - task: TerraformInstaller@1
                  inputs:
                    terraformVersion: $(terraformVersion)

                - task: AWSShellScript@1
                  displayName: 'Terraform Apply'
                  inputs:
                    awsCredentials: 'aws-service-connection'
                    regionName: $(AWS_REGION)
                    inlineScript: |
                      cd $(TF_ROOT)
                      terraform init
                      terraform apply -auto-approve
42.2.2 Terraform Extension

The Azure DevOps Marketplace offers Terraform extensions that provide native tasks for init, plan, validate, and apply operations.

Using Terraform Extension Tasks
Pipeline using the marketplace Terraform task 
# azure-pipelines.yml — Using Terraform Extension
#
# Extension: "Terraform" by Microsoft DevLabs
# Install from: Azure DevOps Marketplace

stages:
  - stage: Deploy
    jobs:
      - job: TerraformDeploy
        pool:
          vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'
        steps:
          # Install specific Terraform version
          - task: TerraformInstaller@1
            displayName: 'Install Terraform v1.7'
            inputs:
              terraformVersion: '1.7.0'

          # Init with backend configuration
          - task: TerraformTaskV4@4
            displayName: 'Terraform Init'
            inputs:
              provider: 'aws'
              command: 'init'
              workingDirectory: '$(System.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/terraform'
              backendServiceAWS: 'aws-backend-connection'
              backendAWSBucketName: 'my-terraform-state'
              backendAWSKey: 'prod/terraform.tfstate'

          # Plan
          - task: TerraformTaskV4@4
            displayName: 'Terraform Plan'
            inputs:
              provider: 'aws'
              command: 'plan'
              workingDirectory: '$(System.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/terraform'
              environmentServiceNameAWS: 'aws-service-connection'
              commandOptions: '-out=tfplan'

          # Apply (manual approval via environment gates)
          - task: TerraformTaskV4@4
            displayName: 'Terraform Apply'
            inputs:
              provider: 'aws'
              command: 'apply'
              workingDirectory: '$(System.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/terraform'
              environmentServiceNameAWS: 'aws-service-connection'
              commandOptions: 'tfplan'
42.2.3 Service Connections

Azure DevOps uses Service Connections to securely authenticate with cloud providers. They support AWS, Azure, and GCP with role-based access.

🔗 Service Connection Types: 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                Azure DevOps Service Connections              │
│                                                              │
│  ┌─── AWS ─────────────┐  ┌─── Azure ─────────────┐        │
│  │ • Access Key/Secret │  │ • Service Principal    │        │
│  │ • Assume Role       │  │ • Managed Identity     │        │
│  │ • OIDC (preview)    │  │ • Workload Identity    │        │
│  └─────────────────────┘  └────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                              │
│  ┌─── GCP ─────────────┐  ┌─── Generic ───────────┐        │
│  │ • Service Account   │  │ • Username/Password    │        │
│  │ • Workload Identity │  │ • SSH Key             │        │
│  │ • OIDC              │  │ • Token               │        │
│  └─────────────────────┘  └────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                              │
│  Config: Project Settings → Service Connections              │
│  Permissions: Pipeline-level or All Pipelines               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Credential Type Rotation Security Recommendation
Access Key + SecretManual⭐⭐Dev/testing only
Assume RoleAuto (STS)⭐⭐⭐⭐Cross-account deploys
OIDC FederationKeyless⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐Production recommended
Service PrincipalManual/Auto⭐⭐⭐⭐Azure deployments

42.3 AWS CodePipeline

PLATFORM 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: AWS CodePipeline

AWS CodePipeline orchestrates Terraform workflows using CodeBuild for execution. This is the fully AWS-native approach — perfect when your infrastructure is entirely on AWS.

42.3.1 CodeBuild Projects

CodeBuild projects execute Terraform commands in isolated containers. Define the build environment, compute type, and IAM role for each project.

CodeBuild Project in Terraform
Defining CodeBuild projects for plan and apply stages 
# codebuild.tf — Terraform Plan & Apply Projects
#
# Separate CodeBuild projects for plan and apply
# to enforce different IAM permissions

resource "aws_codebuild_project" "terraform_plan" {
  name          = "terraform-plan"
  description   = "Terraform plan stage"
  build_timeout = 30
  service_role  = aws_iam_role.codebuild_plan.arn

  artifacts {
    type = "CODEPIPELINE"
  }

  environment {
    compute_type    = "BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL"
    image           = "hashicorp/terraform:1.7"
    type            = "LINUX_CONTAINER"
    privileged_mode = false

    environment_variable {
      name  = "TF_ROOT"
      value = "terraform"
    }

    environment_variable {
      name  = "TF_IN_AUTOMATION"
      value = "true"
    }
  }

  source {
    type      = "CODEPIPELINE"
    buildspec = "buildspec-plan.yml"
  }

  tags = {
    Environment = "shared"
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}

resource "aws_codebuild_project" "terraform_apply" {
  name          = "terraform-apply"
  description   = "Terraform apply stage"
  build_timeout = 60
  service_role  = aws_iam_role.codebuild_apply.arn

  artifacts {
    type = "CODEPIPELINE"
  }

  environment {
    compute_type    = "BUILD_GENERAL1_SMALL"
    image           = "hashicorp/terraform:1.7"
    type            = "LINUX_CONTAINER"
    privileged_mode = false
  }

  source {
    type      = "CODEPIPELINE"
    buildspec = "buildspec-apply.yml"
  }
}
42.3.2 buildspec.yml

The buildspec.yml file defines the build commands for CodeBuild. Create separate buildspecs for plan and apply stages.

Buildspec for Terraform Plan
buildspec-plan.yml with plan output as artifact 
# buildspec-plan.yml — Terraform Plan
#
# Phase 1: Install Terraform (if not using Terraform image)
# Phase 2: Run plan and save output

version: 0.2

env:
  variables:
    TF_ROOT: "terraform"
    TF_IN_AUTOMATION: "true"

phases:
  install:
    runtime-versions:
      python: 3.11
    commands:
      - echo "Using Terraform from container image"

  pre_build:
    commands:
      - cd ${TF_ROOT}
      - terraform init -input=false
      - terraform validate

  build:
    commands:
      - cd ${TF_ROOT}
      - terraform plan -input=false -out=tfplan
      - terraform show -no-color tfplan > plan_output.txt
      - echo "Plan completed successfully"

artifacts:
  files:
    - "${TF_ROOT}/tfplan"
    - "${TF_ROOT}/plan_output.txt"
    - "${TF_ROOT}/**/*"
  discard-paths: no

cache:
  paths:
    - "${TF_ROOT}/.terraform/providers/**/*"
Buildspec for Terraform Apply
buildspec-apply.yml consuming plan artifact 
# buildspec-apply.yml — Terraform Apply
#
# Consumes the plan artifact from the plan stage

version: 0.2

env:
  variables:
    TF_ROOT: "terraform"
    TF_IN_AUTOMATION: "true"

phases:
  pre_build:
    commands:
      - cd ${TF_ROOT}
      - terraform init -input=false

  build:
    commands:
      - cd ${TF_ROOT}
      - terraform apply -input=false -auto-approve tfplan
      - echo "Apply completed successfully"

  post_build:
    commands:
      - cd ${TF_ROOT}
      - terraform output -json > outputs.json
      - echo "Outputs saved"

artifacts:
  files:
    - "${TF_ROOT}/outputs.json"
42.3.3 Cross-Account Deployment

Use CodePipeline with cross-account IAM roles to deploy Terraform across multiple AWS accounts from a central CI/CD account.

🏢 Cross-Account Architecture: 
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CI/CD Account (Central)                │
│                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐              │
│  │ CodePipeline │──▶│  CodeBuild   │              │
│  │              │   │  (Plan/Apply)│              │
│  └──────────────┘   └──────┬───────┘              │
│                            │ sts:AssumeRole        │
└────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┘
                             │
            ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
            ▼                ▼                ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│  Dev Account │  │ Staging Acct │  │  Prod Acct   │
│  111111111   │  │  222222222   │  │  333333333   │
│              │  │              │  │              │
│  IAM Role:   │  │  IAM Role:   │  │  IAM Role:   │
│  terraform-  │  │  terraform-  │  │  terraform-  │
│  deploy      │  │  deploy      │  │  deploy      │
└──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
Cross-Account Pipeline Configuration
CodePipeline with cross-account assume role 
# pipeline.tf — Cross-Account CodePipeline
#
# Central CI/CD account deploys to dev, staging, prod accounts

resource "aws_codepipeline" "terraform" {
  name     = "terraform-infrastructure"
  role_arn = aws_iam_role.codepipeline.arn

  artifact_store {
    location = aws_s3_bucket.pipeline_artifacts.id
    type     = "S3"
  }

  # Source: GitHub webhook
  stage {
    name = "Source"
    action {
      name             = "Source"
      category         = "Source"
      owner            = "AWS"
      provider         = "CodeStarSourceConnection"
      version          = "1"
      output_artifacts = ["source_output"]
      configuration = {
        ConnectionArn    = aws_codestarconnections_connection.github.arn
        FullRepositoryId = "my-org/infrastructure"
        BranchName       = "main"
      }
    }
  }

  # Plan Stage
  stage {
    name = "Plan"
    action {
      name             = "Terraform-Plan"
      category         = "Build"
      owner            = "AWS"
      provider         = "CodeBuild"
      version          = "1"
      input_artifacts  = ["source_output"]
      output_artifacts = ["plan_output"]
      configuration = {
        ProjectName = aws_codebuild_project.terraform_plan.name
      }
    }
  }

  # Manual Approval
  stage {
    name = "Approval"
    action {
      name     = "Manual-Approval"
      category = "Approval"
      owner    = "AWS"
      provider = "Manual"
      version  = "1"
      configuration = {
        NotificationArn = aws_sns_topic.approval.arn
        CustomData      = "Review Terraform plan before applying"
      }
    }
  }

  # Apply Stage
  stage {
    name = "Apply"
    action {
      name            = "Terraform-Apply"
      category        = "Build"
      owner           = "AWS"
      provider        = "CodeBuild"
      version         = "1"
      input_artifacts = ["plan_output"]
      configuration = {
        ProjectName = aws_codebuild_project.terraform_apply.name
      }
    }
  }
}

42.4 CircleCI

PLATFORM 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: CircleCI Documentation

CircleCI provides fast, Docker-based CI/CD with reusable Orbs. Its Terraform orb simplifies pipeline configuration with pre-built commands and executors.

42.4.1 config.yml

CircleCI pipelines are defined in .circleci/config.yml. Use workflows, jobs, and steps to build Terraform automation with approval gates.

CircleCI Terraform Pipeline
Complete config.yml with plan, approval, and apply 
# .circleci/config.yml — Terraform Pipeline
#
# Uses Docker executor with Terraform image
# Workflows provide approval gates between plan and apply

version: 2.1

# ─── Executors ────────────────────────────────────
executors:
  terraform:
    docker:
      - image: hashicorp/terraform:1.7
    working_directory: ~/project/terraform
    environment:
      TF_IN_AUTOMATION: "true"

# ─── Commands (Reusable Steps) ───────────────────
commands:
  tf-init:
    description: "Initialize Terraform"
    parameters:
      environment:
        type: string
        default: "dev"
    steps:
      - run:
          name: Terraform Init
          command: |
            terraform init -input=false \
              -backend-config="key=<< parameters.environment >>/terraform.tfstate"

# ─── Jobs ─────────────────────────────────────────
jobs:
  validate:
    executor: terraform
    steps:
      - checkout:
          path: ~/project
      - tf-init
      - run:
          name: Terraform Format Check
          command: terraform fmt -check -recursive
      - run:
          name: Terraform Validate
          command: terraform validate

  plan:
    executor: terraform
    parameters:
      environment:
        type: string
    steps:
      - checkout:
          path: ~/project
      - tf-init:
          environment: << parameters.environment >>
      - run:
          name: Terraform Plan
          command: |
            terraform plan -input=false \
              -var="environment=<< parameters.environment >>" \
              -out=tfplan
      - persist_to_workspace:
          root: ~/project
          paths:
            - terraform/tfplan
            - terraform/.terraform

  apply:
    executor: terraform
    parameters:
      environment:
        type: string
    steps:
      - checkout:
          path: ~/project
      - attach_workspace:
          at: ~/project
      - run:
          name: Terraform Apply
          command: terraform apply -input=false -auto-approve tfplan

# ─── Workflows ────────────────────────────────────
workflows:
  terraform-deploy:
    jobs:
      - validate

      - plan:
          name: plan-dev
          environment: dev
          requires: [validate]

      - apply:
          name: apply-dev
          environment: dev
          requires: [plan-dev]
          filters:
            branches:
              only: main

      - plan:
          name: plan-prod
          environment: prod
          requires: [apply-dev]
          filters:
            branches:
              only: main

      # Manual approval before production apply
      - hold-prod:
          type: approval
          requires: [plan-prod]

      - apply:
          name: apply-prod
          environment: prod
          requires: [hold-prod]
42.4.2 Orbs

CircleCI Orbs are reusable packages that simplify CI/CD configuration. The Terraform orb provides pre-built commands, executors, and jobs.

Using the Terraform Orb
Simplified pipeline with the CircleCI Terraform orb 
# .circleci/config.yml — Using Terraform Orb
#
# The orb provides: init, plan, apply, destroy commands
# Plus: terraform executor and pre-built jobs

version: 2.1

orbs:
  terraform: circleci/terraform@3.2

# ─── Simple Usage (Orb Jobs) ─────────────────────
workflows:
  deploy-infrastructure:
    jobs:
      # Orb-provided plan job
      - terraform/plan:
          checkout: true
          path: terraform
          workspace: dev
          persist-workspace: true
          context: aws-credentials    # CircleCI context with AWS vars

      # Manual approval gate
      - hold:
          type: approval
          requires:
            - terraform/plan

      # Orb-provided apply job
      - terraform/apply:
          checkout: true
          path: terraform
          workspace: dev
          attach-workspace: true
          requires:
            - hold
          context: aws-credentials
Orb Feature Description Example
terraform/initInitialize command with backend config- terraform/init: { path: terraform }
terraform/planPlan job with workspace persistence- terraform/plan: { workspace: dev }
terraform/applyApply job consuming persisted plan- terraform/apply: { attach-workspace: true }
terraform/fmtFormat check command- terraform/fmt: { path: terraform }
terraform/validateValidate command- terraform/validate: { path: terraform }
💡 CircleCI Contexts: Store AWS credentials in CircleCI Contexts (Organization Settings → Contexts) rather than project-level environment variables. Contexts support security groups, restriction policies, and audit logging. Reference them with context: aws-credentials in workflow jobs.

42.5 Atlantis

TOOL 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Atlantis Documentation

Atlantis is a self-hosted application that automates Terraform workflows via pull request comments. It watches for PRs, runs terraform plan automatically, and applies changes when you comment atlantis apply.

🔱 Atlantis Workflow: 
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     Atlantis Workflow                       │
│                                                            │
│  Developer          GitHub/GitLab        Atlantis Server   │
│     │                    │                     │           │
│     │  Open PR           │                     │           │
│     │───────────────────▶│  Webhook            │           │
│     │                    │────────────────────▶│           │
│     │                    │                     │ terraform │
│     │                    │                     │ plan      │
│     │                    │  PR Comment         │           │
│     │                    │◀────────────────────│           │
│     │  Review plan       │                     │           │
│     │◀───────────────────│                     │           │
│     │                    │                     │           │
│     │  "atlantis apply"  │  Webhook            │           │
│     │───────────────────▶│────────────────────▶│           │
│     │                    │                     │ terraform │
│     │                    │                     │ apply     │
│     │                    │  PR Comment         │           │
│     │                    │◀────────────────────│           │
│     │  Merge PR          │                     │           │
│     │───────────────────▶│                     │           │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
42.5.1 Pull Request Automation

Atlantis automatically runs terraform plan when a PR is opened or updated. The plan output is posted as a PR comment for team review.

Atlantis Installation (Docker)
Running Atlantis as a Docker container 
# Run Atlantis with Docker
#
# Required: GitHub/GitLab webhook configured to send PR events
# Required: GitHub token with repo permissions

docker run -d \
  --name atlantis \
  -p 4141:4141 \
  -e ATLANTIS_GH_USER="atlantis-bot" \
  -e ATLANTIS_GH_TOKEN="ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxx" \
  -e ATLANTIS_GH_WEBHOOK_SECRET="your-webhook-secret" \
  -e ATLANTIS_REPO_ALLOWLIST="github.com/my-org/*" \
  -e AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="AKIA..." \
  -e AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="..." \
  -e AWS_DEFAULT_REGION="us-east-1" \
  ghcr.io/runatlantis/atlantis:latest \
  server \
  --atlantis-url="https://atlantis.example.com" \
  --repo-config="/etc/atlantis/repos.yaml"
PR Comment Commands
Interacting with Atlantis via pull request comments 
# ─── Atlantis PR Comment Commands ─────────────────

# Trigger plan for all modified projects
atlantis plan

# Plan a specific project
atlantis plan -p networking

# Plan with extra Terraform flags
atlantis plan -- -var="instance_type=t3.large"

# Apply the plan (after review + approval)
atlantis apply

# Apply a specific project
atlantis apply -p networking

# Discard the current plan and unlock
atlantis unlock

# Show help
atlantis help

# ─── Example PR Conversation ─────────────────────
#
# @dev-user opened PR #42: "Add RDS Instance"
#
# 🤖 atlantis (bot):
#   Ran Plan for project: **database**
#   ```
#   Terraform will perform the following actions:
#     + aws_db_instance.main
#     + aws_db_subnet_group.main
#   Plan: 2 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.
#   ```
#   * To apply, comment: `atlantis apply -p database`
#
# @reviewer: Looks good! ✅
#
# @dev-user: atlantis apply -p database
#
# 🤖 atlantis (bot):
#   Ran Apply for project: **database**
#   ```
#   Apply complete! Resources: 2 added, 0 changed, 0 destroyed.
#   ```
42.5.2 atlantis.yaml

The atlantis.yaml file at the repo root configures project discovery, custom workflows, and auto-plan triggers.

Repository-Level atlantis.yaml
Configuring projects, workflows, and auto-plan 
# atlantis.yaml — Repository Configuration
#
# Placed at the root of your repository
# Defines projects, workflows, and automation rules

version: 3

automerge: false
parallel_plan: true
parallel_apply: false

projects:
  # ─── Networking Project ─────────────────────────
  - name: networking
    dir: terraform/networking
    workspace: default
    terraform_version: v1.7.0
    autoplan:
      enabled: true
      when_modified:
        - "*.tf"
        - "*.tfvars"
        - "../modules/vpc/**"    # Plan when shared module changes
    apply_requirements:
      - approved              # PR must be approved
      - mergeable             # PR must be mergeable (no conflicts)

  # ─── Application Project ────────────────────────
  - name: application
    dir: terraform/application
    workspace: default
    autoplan:
      enabled: true
      when_modified:
        - "*.tf"
        - "*.tfvars"
    apply_requirements:
      - approved
      - mergeable
      - undiverged            # Branch must be up-to-date with base

  # ─── Database Project ───────────────────────────
  - name: database
    dir: terraform/database
    workspace: default
    workflow: custom-with-tfsec
    autoplan:
      enabled: true
      when_modified:
        - "*.tf"

workflows:
  custom-with-tfsec:
    plan:
      steps:
        - init
        - run: tfsec . --soft-fail
        - plan
    apply:
      steps:
        - apply
42.5.3 Plan and Apply Commands

Atlantis supports custom workflows that extend the default plan/apply steps with additional checks, policy enforcement, and notifications.

Custom Workflow with Policy Checks
Enhanced workflow with security scanning and cost estimation 
# atlantis.yaml — Custom Workflows
#
# Add security scanning and cost estimation to the workflow

workflows:
  enhanced-workflow:
    plan:
      steps:
        # Standard init
        - init

        # Run tfsec security scan
        - run: |
            echo "🔒 Running security scan..."
            tfsec . --format json --out tfsec-results.json || true
            tfsec . --format lovely --soft-fail

        # Run Infracost for cost estimation
        - run: |
            echo "💰 Estimating cost changes..."
            infracost breakdown --path . \
              --format json --out-file infracost.json
            infracost output --path infracost.json \
              --format table

        # Standard plan
        - plan

    apply:
      steps:
        # Verify plan hasn't changed
        - run: echo "🚀 Applying Terraform changes..."
        - apply
        - run: echo "✅ Apply complete!"
42.5.4 Auto-Plan vs Manual

Configure whether Atlantis automatically plans on PR updates or waits for explicit atlantis plan commands.

Feature Auto-Plan Manual Plan
TriggerPR open/updateatlantis plan comment
Best ForSmall teams, fast feedbackLarge teams, cost-sensitive
Resource UsageHigher — plans on every pushLower — plans on demand
Configautoplan: { enabled: true }autoplan: { enabled: false }
File Filteringwhen_modified: ["*.tf"]N/A — manual trigger
🔒 Atlantis Server Security:
  • Webhook Secret: Always validate webhook signatures to prevent unauthorized plan/apply
  • Repo Allowlist: Use --repo-allowlist to restrict which repos can trigger Atlantis
  • Apply Requirements: Require PR approval + mergeable status before apply
  • Command Restrictions: Disable atlantis apply for certain repos in server-side config
  • Network Security: Run Atlantis in a private subnet with ALB/Ingress for webhook exposure only

42.6 Spacelift

PLATFORM 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Spacelift Documentation

Spacelift is an enterprise IaC management platform with built-in OPA policies, drift detection, state management, and RBAC. It supports Terraform, OpenTofu, Pulumi, and CloudFormation.

42.6.1 Stacks

Spacelift organizes infrastructure into Stacks — each stack represents a Terraform project with its own state, configuration, and deployment settings.

Spacelift Stack Configuration (Terraform)
Defining a Spacelift stack using the Spacelift Terraform provider 
# spacelift.tf — Stack Configuration
#
# Manage Spacelift stacks as code using the Spacelift provider

terraform {
  required_providers {
    spacelift = {
      source  = "spacelift-io/spacelift"
      version = "~> 1.0"
    }
  }
}

# ─── VPC Stack ────────────────────────────────────
resource "spacelift_stack" "vpc" {
  name        = "networking-vpc"
  description = "VPC and network infrastructure"
  repository  = "infrastructure"
  branch      = "main"
  project_root = "terraform/networking"

  # Terraform version
  terraform_version = "1.7.0"

  # Auto-deploy on push to main
  autodeploy = true

  # Enable drift detection (check every 15 min)
  terraform_workflow_tool = "TERRAFORM_FOSS"

  labels = ["networking", "production", "aws"]
}

# Environment variables for the stack
resource "spacelift_environment_variable" "aws_region" {
  stack_id   = spacelift_stack.vpc.id
  name       = "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"
  value      = "us-east-1"
  write_only = false
}

# AWS integration (OIDC-based)
resource "spacelift_aws_integration" "prod" {
  name                           = "aws-production"
  role_arn                       = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/spacelift"
  generate_credentials_in_worker = false
  duration_seconds               = 3600
}

resource "spacelift_aws_integration_attachment" "vpc" {
  integration_id = spacelift_aws_integration.prod.id
  stack_id       = spacelift_stack.vpc.id
  read           = true
  write          = true
}

# ─── Application Stack (depends on VPC) ──────────
resource "spacelift_stack" "app" {
  name         = "application-ecs"
  description  = "ECS application infrastructure"
  repository   = "infrastructure"
  branch       = "main"
  project_root = "terraform/application"

  terraform_version = "1.7.0"
  autodeploy        = false    # Manual approval required

  labels = ["application", "production", "aws"]
}

# Stack dependency: app depends on vpc
resource "spacelift_stack_dependency" "app_on_vpc" {
  stack_id            = spacelift_stack.app.id
  depends_on_stack_id = spacelift_stack.vpc.id
}

# Pass VPC outputs to app stack
resource "spacelift_stack_dependency_reference" "vpc_id" {
  stack_dependency_id = spacelift_stack_dependency.app_on_vpc.id
  output_name         = "vpc_id"
  input_name          = "TF_VAR_vpc_id"
}
🔗 Spacelift Stack Dependencies: 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          Spacelift Dependency Graph               │
│                                                   │
│  ┌─────────────┐                                 │
│  │  networking  │  (autodeploy: true)            │
│  │  VPC stack   │──────────┐                     │
│  └─────────────┘          │                      │
│                            │ outputs:             │
│                            │  vpc_id              │
│                            │  subnet_ids          │
│                            ▼                      │
│  ┌─────────────┐   ┌─────────────┐              │
│  │  database   │   │ application │              │
│  │  RDS stack  │   │ ECS stack   │              │
│  └──────┬──────┘   └──────┬──────┘              │
│         │                  │                      │
│         └──────┬───────────┘                      │
│                ▼                                  │
│         ┌─────────────┐                           │
│         │  monitoring │                           │
│         │  CW stack   │                           │
│         └─────────────┘                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
42.6.2 Policies

Spacelift uses Open Policy Agent (OPA) Rego policies to enforce governance rules. Policies can approve, deny, or warn on plans before they're applied.

OPA Policy Examples
Common Spacelift policies in Rego language 
# policies/deny-large-instances.rego
#
# Deny EC2 instances larger than xlarge in dev

package spacelift

# Deny rule — blocks the run
deny["Instance type too large for dev environment"] {
    input.spacelift.stack.labels[_] == "dev"
    resource := input.terraform.resource_changes[_]
    resource.type == "aws_instance"
    instance_type := resource.change.after.instance_type
    not startswith(instance_type, "t3.")
    not startswith(instance_type, "t2.")
}

# Warn rule — allows but flags
warn["Consider using Graviton instances for cost savings"] {
    resource := input.terraform.resource_changes[_]
    resource.type == "aws_instance"
    instance_type := resource.change.after.instance_type
    not contains(instance_type, "g")    # Not a Graviton type
}

# Policy: Require tags on all resources
deny["All resources must have required tags"] {
    resource := input.terraform.resource_changes[_]
    resource.change.after.tags
    not resource.change.after.tags.Environment
    not resource.change.after.tags.ManagedBy
}
Attaching Policies to Stacks
Spacelift policy management via Terraform 
# policies.tf — Spacelift Policy Configuration

resource "spacelift_policy" "deny_large_instances" {
  name        = "deny-large-instances"
  type        = "PLAN"         # Evaluated against terraform plan
  body        = file("policies/deny-large-instances.rego")
  description = "Prevent oversized EC2 instances in dev"

  labels = ["security", "cost"]
}

# Attach policy to all dev stacks via label
resource "spacelift_policy_attachment" "dev_instance_policy" {
  policy_id = spacelift_policy.deny_large_instances.id
  stack_id  = spacelift_stack.vpc.id    # Or use label-based auto-attach
}

# Auto-attach policy to all stacks with "production" label
resource "spacelift_policy" "require_tags" {
  name   = "require-tags"
  type   = "PLAN"
  body   = file("policies/require-tags.rego")

  # Auto-attach to stacks matching these labels
  labels = ["autoattach:production"]
}
Policy Type Evaluated When Use Case
PLANAfter terraform planResource restrictions, cost controls
APPROVALBefore applyRequire specific team approvals
GIT_PUSHOn VCS push eventFilter which pushes trigger runs
LOGINUser loginSSO enforcement, IP restrictions
INITIALIZATIONStack creationEnforce naming conventions
42.6.3 Modules

Spacelift provides a private module registry for sharing Terraform modules across teams. Modules are versioned and can be tested automatically.

Spacelift Module Registry
Publishing and consuming private modules 
# modules.tf — Spacelift Module Configuration

# Register a module in the private registry
resource "spacelift_module" "vpc" {
  name          = "vpc"
  description   = "Standard VPC module with subnets and routing"
  repository    = "terraform-modules"
  branch        = "main"
  project_root  = "modules/vpc"

  # Auto-publish versions from git tags
  terraform_provider = "aws"

  labels = ["networking", "shared"]
}

# ─── Consuming the module ─────────────────────────
# In any stack's Terraform code:
#
# module "vpc" {
#   source  = "spacelift.io/my-org/vpc/aws"
#   version = "~> 2.0"
#
#   cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
#   environment = var.environment
# }
🚀 Spacelift vs Self-Hosted CI/CD:
FeatureSpaceliftGitHub Actions / GitLab CI
State ManagementBuilt-inExternal backend required
Drift DetectionBuilt-in (scheduled)Custom cron job needed
Policy EnforcementOPA / Rego nativeManual integration
RBACFine-grained per stackRepo-level permissions
Cost EstimationBuilt-in InfracostManual Infracost setup
Stack DependenciesNative DAGCustom implementation
PricingPer-seat subscriptionFree (self-managed infra cost)

42.7 env0

PLATFORM 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: env0 Documentation

env0 is a self-service infrastructure platform that empowers developers to deploy environments while maintaining governance. It focuses on cost management, TTL-based temporary environments, and approval workflows.

42.7.1 Environments

env0 environments are deployable instances of infrastructure templates. They support TTL (Time-to-Live), auto-destroy schedules, and budget controls.

env0 Environment Configuration
Creating and managing environments with TTL and cost controls 
# env0.tf — Environment Configuration
#
# Using the env0 Terraform provider

terraform {
  required_providers {
    env0 = {
      source  = "env0/env0"
      version = "~> 0.3"
    }
  }
}

# ─── Project ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "env0_project" "infrastructure" {
  name        = "AWS Infrastructure"
  description = "Production AWS infrastructure"
}

# ─── Template ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "env0_template" "vpc" {
  name              = "VPC Network"
  description       = "Standard VPC with subnets"
  repository        = "https://github.com/my-org/infrastructure"
  path              = "terraform/networking"
  terraform_version = "1.7.0"
  type              = "terraform"

  # Retry on failure
  retries_on_deploy  = 2
  retries_on_destroy = 2
}

# ─── Template Assignment ──────────────────────────
resource "env0_template_project_assignment" "vpc_to_infra" {
  template_id = env0_template.vpc.id
  project_id  = env0_project.infrastructure.id
}

# ─── Environment from Template ────────────────────
resource "env0_environment" "dev_vpc" {
  name        = "dev-vpc"
  project_id  = env0_project.infrastructure.id
  template_id = env0_template.vpc.id

  # Auto-destroy after 8 hours (dev/testing)
  ttl {
    type  = "HOURS"
    value = 8
  }

  # Force destroy even if resources exist
  force_destroy = true

  # Approve automatically for dev
  approve_plan_automatically = true
}

# ─── Configuration Variables ──────────────────────
resource "env0_configuration_variable" "region" {
  name            = "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"
  value           = "us-east-1"
  project_id      = env0_project.infrastructure.id
  is_sensitive    = false
  scope           = "PROJECT"
}

resource "env0_configuration_variable" "env_name" {
  name            = "TF_VAR_environment"
  value           = "dev"
  environment_id  = env0_environment.dev_vpc.id
  scope           = "ENVIRONMENT"
}
⏰ TTL-Based Environment Management: 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          env0 Environment Lifecycle                      │
│                                                          │
│  Create        Active          TTL Warning    Destroy   │
│    │             │                │              │       │
│    ▼             ▼                ▼              ▼       │
│  ┌───┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────┐     │
│  │ 🟢│───▶│  Running  │───▶│ ⚠️ 1hr  │───▶│  🔴  │     │
│  └───┘    │  (TTL:8h) │    │  warning │    │ Auto │     │
│           └──────────┘    └──────────┘    │destroy│     │
│                                           └──────┘     │
│                                                          │
│  Options:                                               │
│  • Extend TTL (developer can request more time)         │
│  • Auto-destroy (clean up resources automatically)      │
│  • Budget alerts (notify when cost threshold reached)   │
│  • Schedule (destroy at end of business day)            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
42.7.2 Templates

env0 Templates define reusable infrastructure blueprints. Developers use templates to self-service deploy environments with guardrails and governance built in.

Template with Custom Flows
env0 template with custom workflow steps 
# env0.yml — Custom Flow Configuration
#
# Placed in the repository root to define custom workflow steps
# These run before/after standard Terraform commands

version: 1

deploy:
  steps:
    setup:
      # Run before terraform init
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          apt-get update && apt-get install -y jq
          pip install checkov

    terraformInit:
      after:
        - name: Verify providers
          run: terraform providers

    terraformPlan:
      before:
        - name: Security scan
          run: |
            checkov -d . --soft-fail \
              --output cli --output junitxml \
              --output-file-path console,results.xml
      after:
        - name: Cost estimation
          run: |
            infracost breakdown --path . \
              --format table

    terraformApply:
      after:
        - name: Smoke test
          run: |
            echo "Running smoke tests..."
            terraform output -json > outputs.json
            # Add your verification scripts here

destroy:
  steps:
    terraformDestroy:
      before:
        - name: Backup state
          run: |
            echo "Backing up state before destroy..."
            terraform state pull > state-backup.json
Platform Best For Unique Feature Pricing Model
SpaceliftEnterprise governanceOPA policy enginePer-seat
env0Self-service & cost controlTTL environmentsPer-seat
AtlantisPR-driven GitOpsOpen source, self-hostedFree (OSS)
Terraform CloudHashiCorp ecosystemSentinel policiesPer-resource
ScalrMulti-tenant managementHierarchical RBACPer-seat
🎯 Choosing Your CI/CD Platform:
  • Already use GitHub? → GitHub Actions (Ch 40) — native integration, free for public repos
  • Already use GitLab? → GitLab CI (Ch 41) — managed state, MR widgets built-in
  • Enterprise with Jenkins? → Jenkins (Section 42.1) — leverage existing infrastructure
  • All-in on Azure? → Azure DevOps (Section 42.2) — tight Azure integration
  • All-in on AWS? → CodePipeline (Section 42.3) — fully native AWS
  • Want GitOps for free? → Atlantis (Section 42.5) — open source, PR-driven
  • Need enterprise governance? → Spacelift (Section 42.6) or env0 (Section 42.7)
☁️ 🏢 🔐 📊
CHAPTER 43

HCP Terraform (Terraform Cloud)

HCP Terraform Remote State Workspaces Sentinel Private Registry Cost Estimation Run Triggers
13Sections
46Subsections
40+Examples
📝Exam Topic
PART 12 ADVANCED ★★★★★

HCP Terraform (formerly Terraform Cloud) is HashiCorp's managed platform for Terraform collaboration. It provides remote state management, policy enforcement, cost estimation, private module registries, and team-based governance — all essential for production infrastructure at scale.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand HCP Terraform tiers, organizations, and workspace types
  • Configure VCS-driven, CLI-driven, and API-driven workflows
  • Integrate GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure DevOps with HCP Terraform
  • Manage remote state with versioning, locking, and access control
  • Use variable sets, run triggers, and private module registries
  • Implement Sentinel policies with enforcement levels and mock testing
  • Configure teams, permissions, cost estimation, and API automation

Chapter Contents

43.1 HCP Terraform Overview 43.2 Organizations 43.3 Workspaces 43.4 VCS Integration 43.5 Remote Operations 43.6 State Management 43.7 Variable Management 43.8 Run Triggers 43.9 Private Registry 43.10 Cost Estimation 43.11 Sentinel Policy as Code 43.12 Teams and Permissions 43.13 API and Automation

43.1 HCP Terraform Overview

CONCEPT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: HCP Terraform Docs

HCP Terraform (formerly Terraform Cloud) is HashiCorp's managed SaaS platform for team-based Terraform workflows. It eliminates the need for self-managed backends, provides centralized state management, and adds governance features like Sentinel and cost estimation.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  HCP TERRAFORM ARCHITECTURE                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────┐     │
│  │Developer │────▶│  VCS (Git)   │────▶│   HCP Terraform     │     │
│  │  (CLI)   │     │  Webhooks    │     │                     │     │
│  └──────────┘     └──────────────┘     │  ┌───────────────┐  │     │
│       │                                │  │ Organization  │  │     │
│       │  terraform login               │  │  ┌──────────┐ │  │     │
│       │  terraform plan                │  │  │Workspace │ │  │     │
│       │  terraform apply               │  │  │ ┌──────┐ │ │  │     │
│       ▼                                │  │  │ │State │ │ │  │     │
│  ┌──────────┐                          │  │  │ │Vars  │ │ │  │     │
│  │ API /    │─────────────────────────▶│  │  │ │Runs  │ │ │  │     │
│  │ Tokens   │                          │  │  │ └──────┘ │ │  │     │
│  └──────────┘                          │  │  └──────────┘ │  │     │
│                                        │  └───────────────┘  │     │
│                                        │                     │     │
│                                        │  ┌─────────┐  ┌──┐ │     │
│                                        │  │Sentinel │  │💰│ │     │
│                                        │  │Policies │  │CE│ │     │
│                                        │  └─────────┘  └──┘ │     │
│                                        └──────────┬──────────┘     │
│                                                   │                │
│                                                   ▼                │
│                                        ┌─────────────────────┐     │
│                                        │   Cloud Providers   │     │
│                                        │  AWS │ Azure │ GCP  │     │
│                                        └─────────────────────┘     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Legend: CE = Cost Estimation
43.1.1 Free vs Team vs Business

HCP Terraform offers multiple pricing tiers. Understanding the differences is essential for the certification exam and for choosing the right plan for your organization.

Feature Free Team (Standard) Team & Governance (Plus) Business
UsersUp to 5UnlimitedUnlimitedUnlimited
WorkspacesUnlimitedUnlimitedUnlimitedUnlimited
Remote State✅✅✅✅
Remote Runs✅✅✅✅
Team Management1 teamMultipleMultipleMultiple
Sentinel Policies❌❌✅✅
Cost Estimation❌❌✅✅
Run Tasks1UnlimitedUnlimitedUnlimited
Private Registry✅✅✅✅
SSO / SAML❌❌❌✅
Audit Logging❌❌❌✅
Custom Agents❌❌❌✅
SupportCommunityStandardStandardPremium
Concurrent Runs133Configurable
📝 Exam Tip: The certification exam frequently tests which features are available at each tier. Sentinel and Cost Estimation require the "Plus" tier. SSO, Audit Logging, and Custom Agents require the "Business" tier.
43.1.2 Feature Comparison

HCP Terraform vs open-source Terraform — a side-by-side comparison of capabilities.

Capability Open Source (CLI) HCP Terraform
State StorageLocal / self-managed S3Managed, encrypted, versioned
State LockingDynamoDB / ConsulBuilt-in automatic
Run ExecutionLocal machineRemote workers
SecretsEnv vars / VaultEncrypted variables
RBACManual / IAMTeams + workspace perms
PolicyExternal toolsSentinel / OPA built-in
Audit TrailNoneFull run history
Module RegistryPublic onlyPrivate + public
Cost EstimationInfracost (3rd party)Built-in
VCS IntegrationManualNative webhooks
43.1.3 When to Use HCP Terraform

Decision framework for choosing between open-source CLI, HCP Terraform, and Terraform Enterprise.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           WHEN TO USE WHICH TERRAFORM EDITION?                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  Team size < 3 ──────────────▶ Open Source CLI                 │
│  Need collaboration? ────────▶ HCP Terraform (Free)            │
│  Need governance? ───────────▶ HCP Terraform (Plus)            │
│  Need SSO / auditing? ──────▶ HCP Terraform (Business)        │
│  Must self-host? ────────────▶ Terraform Enterprise            │
│  Air-gapped network? ───────▶ Terraform Enterprise             │
│  Regulated industry? ───────▶ Enterprise + Custom Agents       │
│                                                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  💡 Most teams start with HCP Terraform Free and upgrade       │
│     as governance needs grow.                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔑 Key Insight: HCP Terraform is the recommended starting point for any team larger than a single developer. The free tier provides remote state, remote runs, and basic team management — which solves the most common Terraform collaboration pain points.

43.2 Organizations

CONCEPT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Organizations

Organizations are the top-level grouping in HCP Terraform. They contain workspaces, teams, policies, variable sets, and registry modules. Every workspace belongs to exactly one organization.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               HCP TERRAFORM HIERARCHY                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Organization: acme-corp                                │
│  ├── Teams                                              │
│  │   ├── platform-team  (admin)                         │
│  │   ├── dev-team       (write on dev-*)                │
│  │   └── security-team  (read + policy manage)          │
│  │                                                      │
│  ├── Workspaces                                         │
│  │   ├── dev-networking     (VCS-driven)                │
│  │   ├── staging-app        (CLI-driven)                │
│  │   ├── prod-networking    (VCS-driven + approval)     │
│  │   └── prod-app           (VCS-driven + approval)     │
│  │                                                      │
│  ├── Variable Sets                                      │
│  │   ├── aws-credentials    (global)                    │
│  │   └── tagging-standards  (applied to prod-*)         │
│  │                                                      │
│  ├── Policy Sets (Sentinel)                             │
│  │   ├── cost-limits        (soft-mandatory)            │
│  │   └── security-baseline  (hard-mandatory)            │
│  │                                                      │
│  └── Registry                                           │
│      ├── acme/vpc/aws       v2.1.0                      │
│      └── acme/ecs-service/aws  v1.3.0                   │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
43.2.1 Organization Setup

Creating and configuring an organization is the first step in adopting HCP Terraform.

Create Organization via tfe Provider
Manage HCP Terraform organizations as code 
# Configure the tfe provider for HCP Terraform
terraform {
  required_providers {
    tfe = {
      source  = "hashicorp/tfe"
      version = "~> 0.54"
    }
  }
}

provider "tfe" {
  # Token set via TFE_TOKEN environment variable
  # Or: token = var.tfe_token
}

# Create an organization
resource "tfe_organization" "acme" {
  name  = "acme-corp"
  email = "platform-team@acme.com"

  # Collaboration settings
  collaborator_auth_policy = "two_factor_mandatory"
  cost_estimation_enabled  = true

  # Session management
  session_timeout_minutes  = 20160  # 14 days
  session_remember_minutes = 43200  # 30 days
}
43.2.2 Team Management

Teams group users and define their access level across workspaces. Each team can have organization-level permissions and workspace-level permissions.

Team Configuration with Permissions
Create teams with varying permission levels 
# Platform/Admin Team — full organization access
resource "tfe_team" "platform" {
  name         = "platform-team"
  organization = tfe_organization.acme.name

  organization_access {
    manage_workspaces   = true
    manage_policies     = true
    manage_vcs_settings = true
    manage_modules      = true
    manage_providers    = true
    manage_run_tasks    = true
  }
}

# Developer Team — limited to specific workspaces
resource "tfe_team" "developers" {
  name         = "dev-team"
  organization = tfe_organization.acme.name
  visibility   = "organization"
}

# Grant workspace-level access to dev team
resource "tfe_team_access" "dev_workspace_access" {
  team_id      = tfe_team.developers.id
  workspace_id = tfe_workspace.dev_app.id

  permissions {
    runs              = "apply"
    variables         = "write"
    state_versions    = "read-outputs"
    sentinel_mocks    = "read"
    workspace_locking = true
    run_tasks         = false
  }
}

# Security Team — read + policy management
resource "tfe_team" "security" {
  name         = "security-team"
  organization = tfe_organization.acme.name

  organization_access {
    manage_policies = true
  }
}
Permission Level Read Plan Apply Admin
View state✅✅✅✅
Queue plans❌✅✅✅
Confirm applies❌❌✅✅
Lock / unlock❌❌✅✅
Edit variables❌❌❌✅
Edit settings❌❌❌✅
Delete workspace❌❌❌✅
43.2.3 Organization Settings

Key organization-wide settings that affect all workspaces and users.

Organization-Wide Settings
Configure notification, authentication, and default settings 
# Organization notification (Slack webhook)
resource "tfe_notification_configuration" "slack" {
  name             = "slack-alerts"
  enabled          = true
  workspace_id     = tfe_workspace.prod_app.id
  destination_type = "slack"
  url              = var.slack_webhook_url

  triggers = [
    "run:needs_attention",
    "run:errored",
    "run:completed"
  ]
}

# Organization token (for CI/CD automation)
resource "tfe_organization_token" "ci" {
  organization = tfe_organization.acme.name
  # Regenerate periodically for security
  force_regenerate = false
}
⚠️ Important: Organization tokens have organization-level access. For CI/CD pipelines, prefer team tokens scoped to specific workspaces. Never use organization tokens in shared pipelines.

43.3 Workspaces

CORE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Workspaces

HCP Terraform workspaces are the primary unit of infrastructure management. Unlike CLI workspaces (which only switch state files), HCP Terraform workspaces each have their own configuration, variables, state, run history, and permissions.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               THREE WORKSPACE EXECUTION MODES                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                    │
│  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐   │
│  │  VCS-DRIVEN     │  │  CLI-DRIVEN     │  │  API-DRIVEN     │   │
│  │  ═══════════    │  │  ═══════════    │  │  ═══════════    │   │
│  │                 │  │                 │  │                 │   │
│  │  Git push ──▶   │  │  terraform ──▶  │  │  API call ──▶   │   │
│  │  Webhook fires  │  │  plan/apply     │  │  Upload config  │   │
│  │  Auto plan      │  │  Runs remote    │  │  Trigger run    │   │
│  │                 │  │                 │  │                 │   │
│  │  Best for:      │  │  Best for:      │  │  Best for:      │   │
│  │  • GitOps       │  │  • Migration    │  │  • Full CI/CD   │   │
│  │  • PR reviews   │  │  • Development  │  │  • Custom tools │   │
│  │  • Auto deploy  │  │  • Transition   │  │  • Pipelines    │   │
│  │                 │  │                 │  │                 │   │
│  │  Trigger:       │  │  Trigger:       │  │  Trigger:       │   │
│  │  VCS webhook    │  │  CLI command    │  │  HTTP POST      │   │
│  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘   │
│                                                                    │
│  📝 All three modes use remote state and remote plan/apply         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
43.3.1 VCS-Driven Workspaces

VCS-driven is the most common mode. Pushing to the connected branch automatically triggers a plan, and merging triggers an apply.

VCS-Driven Workspace Configuration
Connect a workspace to a GitHub repository 
# VCS-driven workspace
resource "tfe_workspace" "prod_networking" {
  name              = "prod-networking"
  organization      = tfe_organization.acme.name
  description       = "Production VPC and networking resources"
  terraform_version = "~> 1.7.0"

  # VCS connection
  vcs_repo {
    identifier         = "acme-corp/terraform-networking"
    branch             = "main"
    oauth_token_id     = tfe_oauth_client.github.oauth_token_id
    ingress_submodules = false
  }

  # Working directory (monorepo support)
  working_directory = "environments/production"

  # Queue all runs (requires manual confirmation)
  queue_all_runs   = false
  auto_apply       = false      # Require manual approval for prod

  # Trigger patterns — only run when these paths change
  trigger_prefixes = [
    "environments/production",
    "modules/vpc",
    "modules/security-groups"
  ]

  # Execution mode
  execution_mode = "remote"

  # Tags for organization
  tag_names = ["production", "networking", "aws"]
}
43.3.2 CLI-Driven Workspaces

CLI-driven workspaces let you use the familiar terraform plan and terraform apply commands while runs execute remotely in HCP Terraform.

CLI-Driven Backend Configuration
Configure local Terraform to use HCP Terraform as backend 
# In your local terraform configuration
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "acme-corp"

    workspaces {
      name = "dev-app"
    }
  }
}

# Alternative: Use tags to match multiple workspaces
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "acme-corp"

    workspaces {
      tags = ["dev", "app"]
    }
  }
}

# ─── CLI workflow ───────────────────────────
# $ terraform login          # Authenticate
# $ terraform init           # Connect to workspace
# $ terraform plan           # Plan runs remotely
# $ terraform apply          # Apply runs remotely
📝 Exam Tip: The cloud block (introduced in Terraform 1.1) replaces the older backend "remote" block. Both work, but cloud is the recommended approach and supports workspace tags.
43.3.3 API-Driven Workspaces

API-driven workspaces provide maximum flexibility for custom CI/CD pipelines. Configuration is uploaded via the API, and runs are triggered programmatically.

API-Driven Workflow
Trigger a run via the HCP Terraform API 
#!/bin/bash
# API-driven workflow — trigger a run via REST API

ORG="acme-corp"
WORKSPACE="staging-app"
TOKEN="${TFE_TOKEN}"
API="https://app.terraform.io/api/v2"

# Step 1: Get workspace ID
WORKSPACE_ID=$(curl -s \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" \
  "${API}/organizations/${ORG}/workspaces/${WORKSPACE}" \
  | jq -r '.data.id')

# Step 2: Create a configuration version
CONFIG_VERSION=$(curl -s \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" \
  --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.api+json" \
  --data '{
    "data": {
      "type": "configuration-versions",
      "attributes": { "auto-queue-runs": false }
    }
  }' \
  "${API}/workspaces/${WORKSPACE_ID}/configuration-versions")

UPLOAD_URL=$(echo ${CONFIG_VERSION} | jq -r '.data.attributes."upload-url"')
CV_ID=$(echo ${CONFIG_VERSION} | jq -r '.data.id')

# Step 3: Upload configuration (tar.gz of .tf files)
tar -czf config.tar.gz -C ./terraform .
curl -s \
  --header "Content-Type: application/octet-stream" \
  --request PUT \
  --data-binary @config.tar.gz \
  "${UPLOAD_URL}"

# Step 4: Create a run
curl -s \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" \
  --header "Content-Type: application/vnd.api+json" \
  --data "{
    \"data\": {
      \"type\": \"runs\",
      \"attributes\": {
        \"message\": \"API-triggered run from CI/CD\",
        \"is-destroy\": false
      },
      \"relationships\": {
        \"workspace\": {
          \"data\": { \"type\": \"workspaces\", \"id\": \"${WORKSPACE_ID}\" }
        },
        \"configuration-version\": {
          \"data\": { \"type\": \"configuration-versions\", \"id\": \"${CV_ID}\" }
        }
      }
    }
  }" \
  "${API}/runs"
43.3.4 Workspace Variables

Variables defined at the workspace level override variable defaults in configuration. They can be Terraform variables or environment variables.

Workspace Variable Configuration
Define Terraform and environment variables for a workspace 
# Terraform variable (appears as var.xxx)
resource "tfe_variable" "instance_type" {
  key          = "instance_type"
  value        = "t3.large"
  category     = "terraform"      # "terraform" or "env"
  workspace_id = tfe_workspace.prod_app.id
  description  = "EC2 instance type for the application"
}

# Sensitive variable (write-only, never displayed)
resource "tfe_variable" "db_password" {
  key          = "db_password"
  value        = var.database_password
  category     = "terraform"
  sensitive    = true
  workspace_id = tfe_workspace.prod_app.id
  description  = "Database master password"
}

# Environment variable (set in the run environment)
resource "tfe_variable" "aws_region" {
  key          = "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"
  value        = "us-east-1"
  category     = "env"
  workspace_id = tfe_workspace.prod_app.id
}

# HCL variable (complex types)
resource "tfe_variable" "tags" {
  key          = "default_tags"
  value        = <<-EOT
    {
      Environment = "production"
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      Team        = "platform"
    }
  EOT
  category     = "terraform"
  hcl          = true
  workspace_id = tfe_workspace.prod_app.id
}
43.3.5 Workspace Tags

Tags organize workspaces and enable workspace selection in the cloud block. They're also useful for applying variable sets and policy sets to groups of workspaces.

Tag-Based Workspace Organization
Use tags to group and filter workspaces 
# Tag workspaces for easy grouping
resource "tfe_workspace" "prod_app" {
  name         = "prod-app"
  organization = tfe_organization.acme.name
  tag_names    = ["production", "app", "aws", "us-east-1"]
  # ...
}

resource "tfe_workspace" "staging_app" {
  name         = "staging-app"
  organization = tfe_organization.acme.name
  tag_names    = ["staging", "app", "aws", "us-east-1"]
  # ...
}

# Select workspace by tags in cloud block
terraform {
  cloud {
    organization = "acme-corp"
    workspaces {
      tags = ["app", "production"]  # Matches prod-app
    }
  }
}

# Apply variable set to all production workspaces
resource "tfe_variable_set" "prod_credentials" {
  name         = "production-aws-creds"
  organization = tfe_organization.acme.name
}

# Scope variable set by workspace tags
# (Done via tfe_workspace_variable_set or project assignment)

43.4 VCS Integration

INTEGRATION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: VCS Integration

VCS integration connects HCP Terraform to your Git repositories. When code is pushed or a PR is opened, HCP Terraform automatically triggers speculative plans (on PRs) or full plan-and-apply runs (on merge).

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              VCS-DRIVEN WORKFLOW                                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│  Developer                  HCP Terraform             Cloud      │
│  ─────────                  ──────────────             ─────      │
│     │                            │                       │       │
│     │  1. Open PR                │                       │       │
│     │─────────────▶ Webhook ────▶│                       │       │
│     │                            │  2. Speculative Plan   │       │
│     │                            │──────────────────────▶│       │
│     │  3. Plan result on PR      │                       │       │
│     │◀───────────── Comment ◀───│                       │       │
│     │                            │                       │       │
│     │  4. Merge PR               │                       │       │
│     │─────────────▶ Webhook ────▶│                       │       │
│     │                            │  5. terraform plan     │       │
│     │                            │──────────────────────▶│       │
│     │                            │  6. Sentinel check     │       │
│     │                            │  7. Cost estimate      │       │
│     │                            │  8. terraform apply    │       │
