  network_mode             = "awsvpc"
  cpu                      = 1024
  memory                   = 2048

  container_definitions = jsonencode([{
    name  = "tfc-agent"
    image = "hashicorp/tfc-agent:latest"
    environment = [
      {
        name  = "TFC_AGENT_TOKEN"
        value = tfe_agent_token.tokens["production"].token
      },
      {
        name  = "TFC_ADDRESS"
        value = "https://tfe.internal.acme.com"
      },
      {
        name  = "TFC_AGENT_NAME"
        value = "ecs-agent"
      },
      {
        name  = "TFC_AGENT_SINGLE"
        value = "false"   # Run continuously
      }
    ]
  }])
}

resource "aws_ecs_service" "tfe_agents" {
  name            = "tfc-agents-prod"
  cluster         = aws_ecs_cluster.main.id
  task_definition = aws_ecs_task_definition.tfe_agent.arn
  desired_count   = 3    # Scale as needed
  launch_type     = "FARGATE"

  network_configuration {
    subnets         = var.private_subnet_ids
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.tfe_agent.id]
  }
}
44.5.2 Agent Scaling

Agents can be scaled horizontally to handle concurrent Terraform operations. Each agent handles one run at a time.

Team Size Concurrent Runs Recommended Agents Agent Spec
Small (1-10 devs)2-53 agents1 vCPU / 2 GB
Medium (10-50 devs)5-158 agents2 vCPU / 4 GB
Large (50-200 devs)15-5020+ agents2 vCPU / 4 GB
Enterprise (200+)50+Autoscale4 vCPU / 8 GB
 
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              AGENT SCALING PATTERNS                            │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                               │
│  FIXED POOL              AUTOSCALING (K8s HPA)                │
│  ──────────              ──────────────────────               │
│                                                               │
│  ┌───────┐               ┌───────┐  min: 2                   │
│  │Agent 1│ ← Always on   │Agent 1│  max: 20                  │
│  │Agent 2│               │Agent 2│  metric: queue depth       │
│  │Agent 3│               │Agent 3│ ← Scaled up               │
│  └───────┘               │Agent 4│   on demand                │
│                          │  ...  │                            │
│  Simple, predictable     └───────┘                            │
│  cost                                                         │
│                          Efficient resource                    │
│                          utilization                           │
│                                                               │
│  SINGLE-USE              MIXED POOLS                          │
│  ──────────              ───────────                          │
│                                                               │
│  TFC_AGENT_SINGLE=true   Pool A: Production (dedicated)       │
│  Agent runs ONE job,     Pool B: Dev/Staging (shared)         │
│  then exits.             Pool C: Air-gapped (specialized)     │
│  → Clean environment                                          │
│     every time           Each pool independently scaled        │
│                                                               │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
44.5.3 Network Requirements

Agents initiate outbound connections to TFE — they do not require inbound connectivity. This simplifies firewall rules and security configurations.

Connection Direction Port Purpose
Agent → TFEOutbound443Job polling, status updates, log streaming
Agent → ProvidersOutboundVariousCloud API access (AWS, Azure, GCP, etc.)
Agent → InternalOutboundVariousPrivate infrastructure (vSphere, databases, etc.)
Agent → RegistryOutbound443Module/provider downloads (or local mirror)
Inbound to Agent❌ None-Agents are pull-based; no inbound ports needed
💡 Key Architecture Point: Agents use a long-polling mechanism to check for new jobs. The agent connects outbound to TFE on port 443 and holds the connection open. When a workspace with execution_mode = "agent" queues a run, TFE responds on the existing connection. This means agents work behind NAT, firewalls, and proxies without any inbound port rules.
🟣
